hero.h1: I'm Jensen, an AI Agent Architect.
hero.subtitle: Exploring the deep integration of LLMs with vertical industries. Grounded in backend architecture and multi-agent orchestration, turning AI capability into real business value.

about.p1: I'm Jensen (刘酝泽). I've designed full-stack pipelines from RAG retrieval to Agent orchestration, and led 0-to-1 projects across ToB products, internal platforms, and vertical-industry systems.
about.p2: I follow Agent design patterns, protocol standards, and inference optimization — and I care more about whether a team can sustain these technologies than about any single piece of cleverness.

skills.h2: From AI Agents to enterprise-grade engineering.
skills.graphNote: Node size = usage depth · color = domain · edges = real relationships

capabilities.h2: Organized by problem type, not chronology.

01.title: RAG & Enterprise Knowledge
01.stance: A knowledge base shouldn't only answer "what" — it should answer "how, in our business".
evidence: Designed retrieval pipelines at ten-thousand-document scale, spanning 6 business domains with millisecond-level recall
Composed LlamaIndex / Haystack / in-house Router into a grey-able chain

02.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
02.stance: An Agent is not a prompt stitched together — it's an engineering design of roles, tools, memory, and termination conditions.
evidence: Shipped ReAct, Plan-Execute, and Multi-Agent Debate patterns in production
Wrapped business services as MCP Tools so Agents stay observable and retryable

03.title: LLM Inference & Engineering
03.stance: I care about the upper bound of model capability, but more about inference cost, controllability, and team-wide usage discipline.
evidence: Brought Qwen / vLLM / Claude Code into the enterprise environment
Drove team-wide adoption of AI coding tools and the Code Review mechanism

04.title: Enterprise Backend Architecture
04.stance: I don't believe "AI projects" can be detached from traditional software engineering. Spring Boot / JVM are the bedrock.
evidence: High-throughput gateways, message middleware, and graph databases carrying real ToB load
End-to-end governance from monolith to microservices, code to CI/CD

05.title: Cross-Domain Delivery
05.stance: I've led 0-to-1 builds in three classes of settings: ToB products, internal platforms, and vertical-industry systems. That range lets me tell quickly whether a request is an engineering problem or a product problem.

01.quote: The hard part is never getting an Agent to run — it's keeping it from hallucinating, timing out, or being bypassed once it's in production.
01.argument: I treat every Agent project as a distributed system: tool calls need timeouts and degradation, context needs boundaries, failures need re-entrant paths. Across 6 business domains, this engineering discipline has been more reliable than any prompt trick.

02.quote: I lean toward subtraction architecture — never use 5 components when 3 will do.
02.argument: I've used LangGraph, CrewAI, Dify, and Coze. The test is always the same: three months from now, can a second person on the team read it, change it, and ship it? Complexity must be inheritable, not showy.

03.quote: The endpoint of a technical decision is not "can we build it" — it's "is it worth maintaining".
03.argument: I've led a company-wide dev platform serving 200+ apps and given 20+ internal tech talks. My belief: an engineer's leverage = the code you write × the code others don't have to write because of you.

cta.p: Open to conversations about enterprise LLM apps, agent orchestration, full-stack engineering, or any interesting project idea.
